lens.included_activities: US independent and regional optical retailers with roughly $1-10 million normalized EBITDA that repeatedly dispense, fit, adjust, repair, warrant, replenish, or otherwise service prescription eyewear and contact lenses for external customers and vision-plan members.
lens.excluded_activities: National or vertically integrated chains, insurer-captive networks, pure online commodity sellers without accountable fitting or service, independent optometry or ophthalmology practices classified in NAICS 621, stand-alone lens manufacturing, single shops below the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers and managed-vision-care payers fund prescription glasses, contacts and related services through retail purchases, insurance allowances, copays, exams where ancillary, warranties and multi-year eye-care clubs; relationships recur with prescription updates, replacement and replenishment.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines nonprescription retail, online sales, prescription fitting and on-premises lens work, while optometry practices are separately classified. The lens is narrowed to independent and regional operators with recurring prescription dispensing, fitting or aftercare so that nonservice sunglasses/readers retail and nontransferable national or captive chains do not dominate the screen.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe optician work rather than a wage-weighted task census for 456130. The occupation includes prescription intake, customer and product advice, work orders, sales records, invoices, insurance submission, inventory and scheduling alongside physical measurement, fitting, adjustment and contact-lens instruction. I assign higher exposure to document, insurance, inventory, marketing and routine recommendation tasks and lower exposure to precise physical service, then temper for technology already deployed at large chains. This may overstate the whole-industry opportunity if opticians are a larger wage share than administrative staff and understate it for digitally immature regional operators.
a.rationale: AI can normalize prescriptions, prepare work orders, check routine completeness, assist benefit estimates and claims, recommend frames and lens options, draft outreach, schedule recalls, forecast inventory, support training and triage service requests. Face measurement, complex fitting, adjustment, repair, contact-lens instruction, final quality judgment and relationship selling remain substantially human.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted multi-site time-and-motion study for independent optical retailers mapping hours to prescription entry, insurance, sales, measurement, fitting, lab coordination, inventory, recall marketing, remakes and adjustments, with current automation penetration reported.
caveats: No six-digit industry occupation mix or direct task-exposure study was found.
National Vision is much larger and more automated than the LMM lens.
The frozen labor ratio is an ancestor 44-45 estimate with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch and IPS harmonization.

rho.rationale: Retail CRM, scheduling, recommendation, inventory and administrative tools can be deployed within five years, and large operators already use integrated POS logic, remote medicine and automation. Realization is limited by legacy optical systems, health and prescription data, managed-care interfaces, precision and remake risk, state licensing, employee training, small IT teams and the need for human acceptance of consequential recommendations.
rho.change_evidence: Audited twelve-month deployments across at least 20 independent or regional stores reporting labor hours per order, insurance denial rate, remake rate, customer complaints, optician override rate, implementation cost and avoided hiring by workflow.
caveats: Vendor capability does not prove staffing reductions in an LMM setting.
Remote eye exams and conventional lab automation are excluded unless AI is the implementation enabler.
State regulation and payer connectivity vary materially.

e.rationale: Prescription dispensing and fitting fit the recurring-service lens, but the EBITDA band likely requires a profitable multi-store group, central lab, professional network or unusually productive single market. Many independent optical shops are too small, national chains are too large, and optometrist-led practices with optical sales are explicitly classified outside 456130.
e.change_evidence: Owner-level Census or commercial data linking 456130 establishments to firms and reporting store count, normalized EBITDA, independent ownership, online share, lab ownership, prescription versus nonprescription mix and optometry-practice classification.
caveats: The frozen firm-count primitive is a declared gap and will be injected as MISSING; this share cannot yield a defensible number of targets.
Co-located eye exams and retail sales create classification ambiguity at the firm and establishment level.
Public-chain economics do not reveal the private-firm EBITDA distribution.

s5.bridge: VSP's announced acquisition of the nearly 250-store Eyemart Express chain and Vision One Credit Union's observation of hundreds of independent optometry practice sale strategies demonstrate an active buyer, lender and succession ecosystem, but neither measures the five-year transfer rate for LMM optical retailers. I use them only as directional evidence and discount for the chain's larger size, the credit union's optometry-practice population, failed sales, closures, minority transactions and classification differences.
s5.rationale: Customer records, payer contracts, trained staff, leases, inventory and labs make an optical platform transferable, and strategic buyers value geographic density. Owner dependence, professional licensure, exam-provider arrangements and managed-care credentialing can delay or prevent a transfer. A one-in-five five-year probability is plausible but not observed.
s5.change_evidence: Closed 2021-2026 US optical-retail transactions from Vision One, Live Oak Bank, VSP Ventures, optical brokers and PitchBook/CapIQ, deduplicated to owner entity and tagged for EBITDA, stores, buyer type, percentage acquired, closure and post-close payer credentialing.
caveats: A single large-chain deal is not representative of LMM annual turnover.
Optometry practice sales may transfer clinical goodwill and belong in NAICS 621 rather than this code.
Internal succession, franchise transfers and minority investments may not qualify.

q.bridge: National Vision's filing measures a national chain, not retention of AI savings by an LMM operator. It reports managed care at 42% of 2025 revenue, estimated payer price concessions, intense competition, and product costs equal to 28.7% of product sales while SG&A was 51.1% of total revenue. I translate the combination of high merchandise contribution and payer/price pressure into moderate benefit retention: greater than cost-plus outsourcing, but materially exposed to allowances, promotions, price competition and service reinvestment. Scale likely gives National Vision better supplier economics but also more price-led positioning than some independents.
q.rationale: Cash-pay product prices and labor savings do not reset automatically, supporting initial retention. Over five years, managed-care fee schedules, vertically integrated competitors, online comparison, promotional pricing, supplier power, customer acquisition and quality or remake reinvestment share the benefit. Differentiated fitting and aftercare retain more than commodity contacts or simple prescriptions.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and order-level cohorts linking workflow savings to later managed-care effective reimbursement, cash price, discounts, lens and frame mix, remakes, warranties, retention and gross margin across two payer-renewal cycles.
caveats: National Vision's national-scale sourcing, private label and payer access differ from LMM operators.
Managed-care penetration varies by local market and target strategy.
This is retained share of implemented gross benefit, not current gross margin.

d5.bridge: BLS projects dispensing-optician employment, not constant-price optical-service quantity: 79,900 jobs in 2024 are projected to reach 82,200 in 2034, a 3% ten-year increase. BLS attributes demand to aging, chronic disease and refractive errors but says online eyewear tempers employment. I convert that occupational outlook into modest five-year service-quantity growth, allowing productivity and online substitution to make quantity grow somewhat faster or slower than employment.
d5.rationale: Corrective eyewear is recurring and often medically necessary. Aging, diabetes and common refractive error support demand, while long replacement cycles, refractive surgery, online migration and affordability limit growth for the current service basket. The result is near-flat to modest real growth rather than a nominal market forecast.
d5.change_evidence: The Vision Council or managed-care claims forecast through 2031 showing inflation- and mix-adjusted exams, complete-pair orders, replacement intervals, contact-lens units, repairs and remakes by independent, chain and online channels.
caveats: Employment is affected by productivity and occupational substitution and is not a direct demand measure.
CDC vision-impairment measures are not the same as demand for correctable eyewear.
New lens technology or quality mix is held constant in this primitive.

o.bridge: BLS directly describes physical fitting, measurement, adjustment, repair and instruction, while National Vision reports only about 7% of 2025 revenue from aggregate omnichannel/e-commerce platforms and notes state regulation of optical dispensing. The FTC Eyeglass Rule simultaneously makes prescriptions portable so customers can shop elsewhere. These sources establish both operator-required work and channel substitution, not the exact future share. I assign high persistence to complex prescription fitting and aftercare and lower persistence to contacts, readers and simple repeat eyeglasses; the estimate may overstate the lens operator's role if remote measurement and direct fulfillment improve quickly.
o.rationale: Customers can research frames, obtain portable prescriptions and reorder contacts online, but precise measurements, fit, adjustment, repair, contact-lens teaching, remake resolution, regulated dispensing and accountable quality remain service functions. An operator persists for most quantity, though it may be a national or digital provider outside the LMM lens.
o.change_evidence: Order-level channel migration through 2031 segmented by prescription complexity, first versus repeat purchase, contact versus glasses, remote measurement use, remake/return rate, state dispensing law and whether local fitting or adjustment occurred.
caveats: National Vision's 7% digital figure includes buy-in-store/ship-to-home and therefore is not pure self-service.
Many states license opticians, not all; rules and enforcement vary.
Operator-required does not guarantee the operator remains an independent regional retailer.

narrative.executive_view: Optical retail fits the recurring-service lens better than ordinary retail because prescription intake, fitting, adjustment, warranties and contact replenishment create accountable repeat work. AI can remove administrative and recommendation hours, but precision, regulation and physical aftercare constrain substitution. The investable population is narrowed by the exclusion of optometry practices and national chains, and commercial retention depends heavily on managed-care and online competition.
narrative.ai_impact: High-value uses include prescription data capture, order completeness checks, benefit and claim support, recall scheduling, routine customer service, frame and lens recommendations, CRM personalization, inventory forecasting, training and lab routing. Large chains already automate routing and use remote-care and integrated systems. The operator must preserve human measurement, fitting, adjustment, teaching, exception handling and final quality accountability to avoid remakes and clinical or reputational harm.
narrative.value_capture: Cash-pay merchandise and private-label economics allow an operator to keep initial labor savings, but managed-care programs account for a substantial share of industry revenue and can impose concessions. Vertically integrated suppliers, insurers and retailers, along with prescription portability and online price comparison, create leakage. Better-fitting, complex and aftercare-heavy work should retain more benefit than commodity contacts or basic repeat orders.
narrative.firm_availability: Independent stores and practice-adjacent opticals are common, yet most single locations likely fall below the EBITDA band and many optometrist-owned practices belong to NAICS 621. Viable targets are regional optical groups, central-lab platforms, professional networks and multi-store operators. Strategic acquisitions and specialist succession finance show transferability, but no defensible lens-specific transaction rate or firm count exists.
narrative.demand_durability: Aging, chronic disease and widespread refractive errors support recurring corrective-eyewear demand. BLS expects modest optician employment growth while explicitly noting online purchasing as a brake. Physical fit, adjustment and quality resolution preserve a majority operator role, but prescription portability, remote care and direct fulfillment can move that role away from local LMM retailers.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal uncertainties are the missing LMM firm count, owner-level EBITDA distribution, classification between retail and optometry practice, managed-care contract terms, supplier concentration, state licensure, online migration, remote refraction, AI error, remakes and availability of optometrists or licensed opticians. A poor target is a store group whose profits depend on favorable payer or supplier terms that do not survive a change of control.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring prescription fulfillment combines data-heavy administration with physical fitting and aftercare, creating AI productivity without eliminating accountable service.
narrative.principal_weakness: Managed-care and vertically integrated competitors can capture savings, while many independent optical businesses are too small or classified as optometry practices outside the code.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: 456130 includes retailing and fitting prescription eyewear, on-premises lens grinding with retail and nonprescription eyewear; independent optometry and ophthalmology practices are classified in NAICS 621.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Opticians
sources.supports: Optician duties, physical and administrative work, licensure variation, 2024 employment and wages, 3% 2024-34 outlook, and demand drivers including aging, chronic disease, refractive error and online substitution.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Opticians, Dispensing
sources.supports: Dispensing-optician work includes health service, office administration, accounting, physical labor, sales, teaching and professional advising.

sources.title: National Vision Holdings, Inc. Form 10-K for Fiscal Year Ended January 3, 2026
sources.supports: Recurring plan and service revenue, 42% managed-care revenue, product and SG&A economics, online share, store and lab automation, competition, regulation, AI and remote-care risks.

sources.title: FTC Announces Final Eyeglass Rule Updates
sources.supports: Prescribers must automatically release eyeglass prescriptions without tying release to an eyewear purchase, facilitating shopping and retailer competition.

sources.title: CDC Fast Facts: Vision Loss
sources.supports: US aging, chronic-condition and vision-risk context: roughly 7 million people with vision impairment and 93 million adults at high risk for serious vision loss.

sources.title: VSP Vision Enters Into Definitive Agreement to Acquire Eyemart Express
sources.supports: Direct evidence of a qualifying strategic optical-retail control transaction involving a nearly 250-store US chain, though above the LMM lens.

sources.title: Vision One Credit Union: How to Maximize the Value of Your Practice
sources.supports: A specialist lender reports observing hundreds of independent optometry practice sale strategies and describes financing and valuation based on adjusted cash flow.
